text: Biomechanical and musculoskeletal measurements are not generally supported as risk factors for running-related injury in non-elite runners based on current evidence.
Lower knee extension strength is associated with increased risk of running-related injury, though the effect size is trivial to small.
Lower hip adduction velocity is associated with increased risk of running-related injury, though the effect size is small and based on limited studies.
Over 100 discrete biomechanical and musculoskeletal variables tested across 30 prospective studies showed no consistent relationship with running-related injury incidence.
Injury prevention recommendations for non-elite runners cannot be reliably based on biomechanical or musculoskeletal measurements alone.
Atypical lower limb alignment and mechanical function have not been confirmed as meaningful predictors of running-related injury despite being commonly proposed risk factors.
